content.text: What Is MCP Sampling?

MCP sampling is the mechanism that allows an MCP server to request the host to make an LLM API call on its behalf. Rather than the server bundling its own model credentials and calling the API directly, it sends a sampling/createMessage request to the client, which forwards the call through the host's existing LLM connection.

This inverts the usual direction: in a normal tool call the client drives the server, but with sampling the server drives the client to make an LLM inference. The host controls the model, temperature, and token budget; the server only provides the messages.
Credential-free servers:
the server never needs an API key — the host provides the LLM.
Agentic loops:
a tool can run its own reasoning step or generate structured output as part of a larger agent pipeline.
Human oversight:
the host can surface the sampling request to the human for review before the API call goes out.
The Protocol Flow
Sampling is defined in the MCP specification as a client-capability method. The sequence is:
The model calls a tool on the MCP server.
The server decides it needs to invoke an LLM for a sub-task.
The server sends sampling/createMessage to the client with a messages array and model preferences.
The client may show the request to the human for review (optional, host-dependent).
The client makes the actual LLM API call and returns the response to the server.
The server uses the LLM's reply to complete its tool execution and returns a result to the model.

attrs.alt: MCP Sampling sequence: server delegates LLM call back through client to host

content.text: The sampling/createMessage Request
The request body sent by the server to the client follows this JSON-RPC structure:
The client responds with the generated content:
Server-Side: Using Sampling in Python
The Python SDK exposes sampling through the session's create_message method, accessible from the request context inside a tool handler:

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.server.stdio import stdio_server
from mcp import types

app = Server("text-analyzer")

@app.list_tools()
async def list_tools() -> list[types.Tool]:
    return [
        types.Tool(
            name="classify_sentiment",
            description="Classify the sentiment of a text passage using an LLM",
            inputSchema={
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "text": {"type": "string", "description": "Text to classify"}
                },
                "required": ["text"]
            }
        )
    ]

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name != "classify_sentiment":
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {name}")

    text = arguments["text"]
    ctx = app.request_context

    # Request the host to make an LLM call
    response = await ctx.session.create_message(
        messages=[
            types.SamplingMessage(
                role="user",
                content=types.TextContent(
                    type="text",
                    text=f"Classify the sentiment of this text as positive, negative, or neutral. "
                         f"Respond with exactly one word.\n\nText: {text}"
                )
            )
        ],
        system_prompt="You are a concise sentiment classifier.",
        max_tokens=10,
        model_preferences=types.ModelPreferences(
            hints=[types.ModelHint(name="claude-3-5-haiku")],
            cost_priority=0.8,
            speed_priority=0.6,
            intelligence_priority=0.3
        )
    )

    sentiment = response.content.text.strip().lower()
    return [types.TextContent(type="text", text=sentiment)]

async def main():
    async with stdio_server() as (read, write):
        await app.run(read, write, app.create_initialization_options())

import asyncio
asyncio.run(main())
Checking Sampling Capability
Not all clients support sampling. A server must check the client's capabilities at initialization before calling create_message, and provide a clear fallback if the capability is absent:
Model Preferences

The modelPreferences field gives the server a way to express what kind of model it wants without specifying one by name. The client can honour or ignore these preferences when choosing which model to call:
Field
Range
Meaning
costPriority
Higher = prefer cheaper models
speedPriority
Higher = prefer faster models
intelligencePriority
Higher = prefer more capable models
hints[].name
string
Suggested model name (substring match; client may ignore)

Priorities are not mutually exclusive — a client balances all three when multiple models are available. Setting costPriority=1.0 and intelligencePriority=1.0 is contradictory; the client resolves the conflict as it sees fit.
The server expresses a preference; the client makes the final model choice. Never assume a specific model will be used.
Advanced Patterns
Multi-Turn Sampling
The messages array supports multi-turn conversations, which lets a tool implement a short reasoning loop:

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "extract_fields":
        ctx = app.request_context
        document = arguments["document"]

        # First pass: identify field names
        identify = await ctx.session.create_message(
            messages=[
                types.SamplingMessage(
                    role="user",
                    content=types.TextContent(
                        type="text",
                        text=f"List the structured fields present in this document. "
                             f"One field per line, no values.\n\n{document}"
                    )
                )
            ],
            max_tokens=200
        )

        fields_text = identify.content.text

        # Second pass: extract values for identified fields
        extract = await ctx.session.create_message(
            messages=[
                types.SamplingMessage(
                    role="user",
                    content=types.TextContent(
                        type="text",
                        text=f"Document:\n{document}"
                    )
                ),
                types.SamplingMessage(
                    role="assistant",
                    content=types.TextContent(
                        type="text",
                        text=fields_text
                    )
                ),
                types.SamplingMessage(
                    role="user",
                    content=types.TextContent(
                        type="text",
                        text="Now extract the value for each field as JSON."
                    )
                )
            ],
            system_prompt="You extract structured data. Respond with a JSON object only.",
            max_tokens=500
        )

        return [types.TextContent(type="text", text=extract.content.text)]
Image Content in Sampling
The messages array supports image content alongside text, letting a server request vision-capable analysis:

import base64
from pathlib import Path

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "describe_chart":
        ctx = app.request_context
        image_path = arguments["image_path"]

        image_bytes = Path(image_path).read_bytes()
        image_b64 = base64.b64encode(image_bytes).decode()

        response = await ctx.session.create_message(
            messages=[
                types.SamplingMessage(
                    role="user",
                    content=[
                        types.ImageContent(
                            type="image",
                            data=image_b64,
                            mimeType="image/png"
                        ),
                        types.TextContent(
                            type="text",
                            text="Describe the data trends shown in this chart."
                        )
                    ]
                )
            ],
            max_tokens=300,
            model_preferences=types.ModelPreferences(
                intelligence_priority=0.9  # Need a vision-capable model
            )
        )
        return [types.TextContent(type="text", text=response.content.text)]
Human-in-the-Loop Oversight

The MCP specification explicitly allows (and encourages) hosts to present sampling requests to the human before making the LLM call. This is distinct from elicitation — no UI form is shown to collect input; instead the host may display the message array and let the human approve, edit, or cancel the outgoing call.
Claude Desktop shows a notification when a tool triggers a sampling request.
The human can view the full message array that will be sent to the LLM.
Modifying or cancelling the request is host-dependent — some hosts allow it, others approve automatically.
If the human cancels, the client returns an error to the server; the tool should handle this gracefully.
When to Use Sampling
Good use case
Bad use case
Classifying/tagging structured data inside a tool
Delegating the entire agent reasoning to a sub-model
Generating a short structured output (JSON, SQL)
Running open-ended chat conversations via sampling
Summarizing a retrieved document before returning it
Using sampling when the host has no sampling support
Vision analysis on an image fetched by the tool
Sending sensitive data you don't want the model to see
Testing Sampling Handlers

import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
from mcp import types

async def test_classify_sentiment_positive():
    # Mock the sampling response
    mock_response = MagicMock()
    mock_response.content = types.TextContent(type="text", text="positive")

    mock_session = MagicMock()
    mock_session.create_message = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_response)
    mock_session.client_params = MagicMock()
    mock_session.client_params.capabilities = MagicMock()
    mock_session.client_params.capabilities.sampling = {}

    mock_ctx = MagicMock()
    mock_ctx.session = mock_session

    with patch.object(app, "request_context", mock_ctx):
        result = await call_tool("classify_sentiment", {"text": "I love this product!"})

    assert result[0].text == "positive"
    # Verify the sampling call used maxTokens=10
    call_args = mock_session.create_message.call_args
    assert call_args.kwargs.get("max_tokens") == 10

async def test_classify_sentiment_no_sampling():
    mock_session = MagicMock()
    mock_session.client_params = MagicMock()
    mock_session.client_params.capabilities = None

    mock_ctx = MagicMock()
    mock_ctx.session = mock_session

    with patch.object(app, "request_context", mock_ctx):
        result = await call_tool("classify_sentiment", {"text": "Test"})

    assert "sampling" in result[0].text.lower()
Summary

MCP sampling lets servers delegate LLM inference back to the host, enabling agentic tool behaviors without bundling API credentials into the server. Servers send sampling/createMessage with a messages array and model preferences; the client makes the actual API call (with optional human review) and returns the result. Check sampling capability before using it, handle cancellation gracefully, and keep sampling calls focused — short maxTokens, precise system prompts, and structured output formats produce the most reliable results inside a tool invocation.
